chat.author: Alexander Pelov
chat.text:
Prefixes compress really well with SCHC.
Having this per-celestial body allocation can lead to a very static address space allocation..
which works beautifully with SCHC

chat.author: Alexander Pelov
chat.text: in terms of bytes over the air, IPv6+SCHC would probably outperform IPv4

chat.author: Jorge Amodio
chat.text: wrong

chat.author: Alexander Pelov
chat.text: wrong on which point, sorry?

chat.author: Éric Vyncke
chat.text: @Alex I was thinking the same, especially has many 'slow link' are P2P

chat.author: Jorge Amodio
chat.text: that IPv6+SCHC will work better in deep space

chat.author: Padma Pillay-Esnault
chat.text: One aspect we need to be conscious of is some mechanisms used take for granted that it is easy to update and signaling changes which is far from true in space

chat.author: Jorge Amodio
chat.text: and that there is no "routing protocol" for deep space

chat.author: Marc Blanchet
chat.text: @jorge there are implementations of IPv6+SCHC that proves Alexander point. So I don't understand what you are saying is wrong.

chat.author: Peter Koch
chat.text: +1 Lars, IGF as one example, but not with a blank sheet.; the design will attract attention from governments as soon as space agencies get involved, so that outreach is needed; however, starting a process with a blank sheet of paper is unlikely to lead to useful results;

chat.text: @marc there is no active deep space mission using IPv6

chat.author: Jorge Amodio

chat.text: @padma - Clarification question - what would the point for the side meeting be?

chat.author: Éric Vyncke

chat.author: Alexander Pelov
chat.text:
SCHC was designed, used and deployed in places that are quite similar to deep space in some aspects.
There are of course things to be concious about.
But deep space is not the only place where software updates, or communications are difficult
Actually, the first deployments lacked any notion of update (software or whatever) and they worked for years (I'm talking about thousands of smart meters for critical infrastructure for example).

chat.author: Padma Pillay-Esnault
chat.text: We will organize an interim meeting on this specific topic

chat.text: +1 to what Alexander said

chat.author: Wesley Eddy

chat.author: Jorge Amodio
chat.text: @alex true but reality and economics of deep space are quite different

chat.author: Padma Pillay-Esnault
chat.text: +1 Jorge

chat.author: Éric Vyncke
chat.text: IPv4 on a slide ....

chat.author: Jorge Amodio
chat.text: you need to include micro-disruptions on the links in each directiion

chat.author: Jorge Amodio
chat.text: links are highly asymetrical

chat.author: Jorge Amodio
chat.text: you may even have the forward link on a ground station and the return link in a different one

chat.author: Jorge Amodio
chat.text: in that case this simulation is not helpful

chat.author: Alexander Pelov
chat.text:
I was about to agree with Jorge about this, but then again -
When you deploy say 1M smart meters, it takes around 10 years until the utility issues the tender, selects the winner(s), rolls out small-scale tests, runs them for year(s), then rolls out the full deployment
The cost of such deployment is around 100M$ (for the hardware and the people that have to go and actually do the 1M meter deployments)
And then the infrastructure needs to be supported for 15-20 years.
If there is a problem, you may affect 1M households at the same time
If you have to send a technician to fix sometihng that would take 1M visits, if you schedule 1/2 day per visit, that's 500k visits
I'd say economics is definitely different, but it doesn't mean it is less critical, or cheaper 
anyways, this is probably not the place to have this discussion, I'd be open to discuss in a side meeting or other place

chat.author: Padma Pillay-Esnault
chat.text: there is also an important consideration in space is that it has power constraints, the top priority is the safety of the space vehicle not routing. We do not experience such constraints on typical terrestrial network or even in IOT as these do not have the same safety nor economic issues

chat.author: Padma Pillay-Esnault
chat.text: sensors may go away not the flight ability of a vehicle ...

chat.author: Jorge Amodio
chat.text: @alex one very popular acronym used in aerospace SWAP

chat.author: Padma Pillay-Esnault
chat.text: or even the ability to recharge

chat.author: Jorge Amodio
chat.text: agreed

chat.author: Padma Pillay-Esnault
chat.text: FWIW launch cost is very high and are limited to amount of vehicles they can place is orbit... they are so many other things that really make these not directly comparable

chat.author: Jorge Amodio
chat.text: one recommendation would be to model the links based in actual performance from space links, like what Teresa did in her thesis

chat.author: Padma Pillay-Esnault
chat.text: $and launches are limited

chat.author: Jorge Amodio
chat.text: yup

chat.author: Jorge Amodio
chat.text: there is a good larticle from IEEE talking about the reality of millions of flying datacenters ...

chat.author: Jorge Amodio
chat.text: Teresa Algarra did similar work

chat.author: Alexander Pelov
chat.text:
well, I love these discussions, because it is getting in the details of how things work
On several radio comms we've shown that using SCHC reduces the energy required (actually, compressing a byte with SCHC is always more efficient than sending it over the air) - not sure how that works in space
In any case, I agree with you that things must be done carefully
I'm simply pointing out that we should probably have a short dedicated session on SCHC and show how it addresses all the different constraints

chat.author: Joerg Ott
chat.text: Concerning implementations: you can talk to Natia (and her ANRW paper that looked at Quinn and quiche) and ask her about differences

chat.author: Jorge Amodio
chat.text: space links are hardly bidirectional

chat.author: Jorge Amodio
chat.text: orbital dyanmics are not your friend

chat.author: Padma Pillay-Esnault
chat.text: @Jorge please elaborate on bidirectional. Orbital dynamics will typically require store and forward ...

chat.author: Jorge Amodio
chat.text: store and forward in some cases is a must

chat.author: Padma Pillay-Esnault
chat.text: of  course but i do not get the no bidirectional - did you mean p2p or multi hop?

chat.author: Jorge Amodio
chat.text: when you talk about RTT like for example Mars, when you get the response earth will be in a different rotation, you nay be receiving the response in a different antenna

chat.author: Jorge Amodio
chat.text: there is no such thing like p2p with Mars

chat.author: Padma Pillay-Esnault
chat.text: that is not bidirectional you mean asymmetric latency

chat.author: Padma Pillay-Esnault
chat.text: you can have a p2p with an orbital relay ..

chat.author: Jorge Amodio
chat.text: and obviously it gets worse if you go beyond

chat.author: Padma Pillay-Esnault
chat.text: not having context is harder to interpret ...

chat.author: Jorge Amodio
chat.text: @padma there is no "link" end t end in both directions

chat.author: Jorge Amodio
chat.text: you schedule the passes and task the ground stations for a forward link so you can transmitt

chat.author: Jorge Amodio
chat.text: in parallel you task the samne or other ground station for the return link to receive

chat.author: Jorge Amodio
chat.text: it is two separate links

chat.author: Jorge Amodio
chat.text: always

chat.author: Jorge Amodio
chat.text: and the asymetric issue is related to power and size of antennas

chat.author: Padma Pillay-Esnault
chat.text: yes it is possible but not always

chat.author: Wesley Eddy

chat.author: Jorge Amodio
chat.text: if it is a moving spacecraft you need to add the tracking component

chat.author: Jorge Amodio
chat.text: all that is related to orbital dynamics

chat.author: Padma Pillay-Esnault

chat.text: QUIC in space is no longer QUIC

chat.text: Related to Lars: Besides talking to QUIC on Earth, you might also have entities on other planets (rovers, etc.) talking to each other via QUIC
